You are fighting the Shalin Hunter. From the waist down, he has the body of the sort of bird that murders wood-based rodents before they even knew it was there: tawny wings, fluffy white under-barrel with touches of brown, and thick, downy claws tipped with brutal black talons. Except this bird is big enough to make you the mouse. From the waist up, he has a bare, recognizably human body: slim, pale, almost elfin in his lithe long arms and high, feathery ears. Silken, shoulder-length black hair is held back from his face with a headband, and a similarly black bandana emblazoned with a white Kihan cog masks his lower face. Above it, round, yellow, unblinking eyes follow you wherever you go. They are devoid of mercy, or rage, or malice. You don’t feel any of these things towards prey. So far, so standard shalin. What is unusual are the growths that protrude from his upper shoulders: five foot long prehensile appendages that become tufted with tawny feathers towards their squid-like spade-pads. Their undersides are covered in tiny, pink, feather-like nubs. He keeps them curled up like fern buds most of the time, but when he extends them, they writhe, seemingly of their own lurid intent, and their ends crackle with electricity, generated by the avian somehow. Perhaps the gleam of brass, straight-edged, biomechanical augmentation you can see running along their flanks provides a clue. You are used to being deafened by shalinae. This one does not make a sound, except the wintery rush of wind over his wings as he swoops towards you, and the occasional static snap of his tentacles. There’s a spare, silent grace with which he notches and fires the longbow he is armed with, even in mid-air. This is much worse than the shrieking harpies.

Through the wild tangle of bush and branch, a faint, melodious sound pricks your ears. Is someone singing? You pick your way through the undergrowth towards it.

Though the song gets louder, loud enough for you to recognize it as a woman’s voice, you cannot pinpoint its source. It seems to be coming from up above, somewhere high in the branches. It’s very still in this part of the forest. You pause for a moment and listen.

[var0
	|It’s a minor key song, a lament with a bitter, tempestuous undercurrent. You’re put in mind of someone singing on a cliff over the sea that swallowed her husband. Your babel software struggles with the translation; there are layers of nuance and ancient form here it cannot parse, and whoever is singing belongs to a race only recently discovered. However, it gives you the gist. 
	
	Somewhere high above, a mother is singing to her as-yet-unborn child. She is telling them they are the progeny of a forced union with one of her people’s enemies, one she deeply resents. She promises that this will not stop her loving the child. She will raise them smart and strong in the traditions of her people, and when the time is right she will direct the child right back at the enemy. A righteous revenge will follow.
	
	It’s certainly moving, in its own haunting way. The antagonist in that song better watch their back, whoever that might be!
